A deeper post has more lateral resistance because the soil column supporting it is longer, distributing the rocking force over more contact area.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand the soil mechanics. I just know deeper gate posts don't sag.

entries.intro: My mother told me: gratitude turns what we have into enough. It's not about having more: it's about recognizing what's here.
details: She started every meal by naming something she was thankful for. The habit made her the most contented person I've known.
I've tried it myself and the effect is real. Naming what you're grateful for changes how you see everything else.
reactions: I practice gratitude daily. It costs nothing and changes everything.

entries.intro: Being a {self_role} has taught me that most problems solve themselves if you give them a day. The ones that don't are the ones worth your attention.
details: I used to jump on every issue the moment it appeared. Now I wait. Half of them are gone by morning.
The urgent problems that persist past a day of patience are the real ones. The rest were just noise.
reactions: I give problems a day before I act. It saves me from solving things that weren't actually broken.

entries.intro: My grandfather said: you can't build a reputation on what you're going to do. Only on what you've done.
details: He judged people and himself by completed work, never by promises.
Intentions are invisible. Results are not. He taught me to measure by what's finished.
reactions: I finish what I start. That's where reputation lives.

entries.intro: Dry mint leaves and scatter them where mice come in. My aunt's pantry was the only one in the neighborhood without mice.
details: She grew mint along the house wall and dried it in bundles. Crushed leaves at every gap and corner.
The smell is pleasant to us but the mice won't cross it. She called it the gentlest fence.
reactions: I use mint instead of traps. It keeps them out without the mess.

statCheck.pass: Mint contains menthol and pulegone, compounds that irritate the sensitive nasal passages of rodents. Mice navigate primarily by scent, and a strong mint barrier overwhelms their olfactory system, making the area unnavigable. The effect is a chemical fence that repels without physical harm.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what in the mint they don't like. I just know they won't go near it.

entries.intro: My father always told me: it's not the load that breaks you. It's the way you carry it.
details: He carried heavy burdens with steady shoulders and never complained. Said posture and attitude were the same thing.
I've seen people crumble under small loads and stand tall under enormous ones. The difference is always the carrying.
reactions: I adjust how I carry things, not how much. That's where the endurance lives.

entries.intro: If the woolly caterpillars have wide brown bands, the winter will be mild. Narrow bands mean cold. My grandmother read them like a calendar.
details: She'd pick one up in autumn and inspect the coloring. Wide brown meant lighter firewood stacking.
I don't know if it's truly predictive, but her forecasts based on caterpillars matched the winter more often than not.
reactions: I check the caterpillars for the fun of it. Whether it's science or superstition, the pattern holds often enough.

statCheck.pass: The brown band width on woolly bear caterpillars corresponds to the caterpillar's age and the conditions of the preceding growing season, not a true winter prediction. But because the same conditions that produce a strong growing season tend to precede a mild winter, the correlation appears to hold.
statCheck.fail: I'm not sure the caterpillars really know. But my grandmother believed it and she was rarely wrong.

entries.intro: My aunt always said: not every battle is worth fighting, but every battle worth fighting is worth winning.
details: She was selective about her conflicts but relentless in the ones she chose. Half the wisdom was in the choosing.
I've wasted energy on fights that didn't matter. The ones that mattered, I made sure to finish.
reactions: I choose my battles carefully and commit fully to the ones I pick.

entries.intro: Store your flour in a sealed jar with a dried bay leaf and it'll keep for months without going stale or wormy. My mother never lost a bag.
details: She transferred every bag of flour into ceramic jars the day she bought it. Said cloth sacks invite moths.
The seal keeps the moisture stable and the bay leaf keeps the insects out. Simple as that.
reactions: I jar my flour the day I buy it. The habit has saved me more food than I can count.

entries.intro: My grandmother used to say: pride is not the opposite of shame. They're the same coin. True humility is the middle path.
details: She lived without boasting and without self-pity. Said both were ways of making yourself the center of attention.
The proudest and the most ashamed people are both obsessed with themselves. The humble ones are free.
reactions: I aim for the middle. Not proud, not ashamed, just steady.

entries.intro: If the cows lie down in the field, rain is on the way. My grandfather said they can feel the drop in pressure through their legs.
details: He watched the herd from the hill every morning. Standing cows meant fair weather. Lying cows meant fetch the cloak.
It's not perfect: sometimes they're just tired. But when the whole herd goes down at once, the rain follows.
reactions: I check the cows when I pass a field. They're right more often than they're wrong.

statCheck.pass: Cows may lie down in response to dropping barometric pressure or increasing humidity, which can cause joint discomfort and a preference for resting. The behavior also conserves body heat: lying on dry ground before rain keeps a warm, dry spot reserved. It's likely a comfort response rather than true prediction.
statCheck.fail: I can't explain why cows know. I just know lying cows usually mean rain.

entries.intro: My mother said: don't be so busy making a living that you forget to make a life.
details: She worked hard but she stopped when the day was done. Said rest was part of the work, not the enemy of it.
I've known people who built fortunes and lost their families. She built a home first and the rest filled in around it.
reactions: I work to live, not the other way around. The distinction matters.

entries.intro: Drive a wedge into the butt end of a log before splitting and you'll guide the crack where you want it. My uncle controlled every split.
details: He'd tap a small iron wedge into the end grain, then swing the maul beside it. The wood opened along the line he chose.
Without the wedge, the crack wanders to every knot and weakness. With it, you decide where the wood breaks.
reactions: I use a wedge on anything difficult. A little guidance turns a struggle into a clean break.

entries.intro: My father told me: the things you're most afraid of have already happened to someone who survived them. Fear is rarely as big as it looks.
details: He faced everything calmly because he reminded himself that someone before him had faced worse.
Perspective is the best antidote to fear. What seems impossible is usually just unfamiliar.
reactions: I remind myself that my fears are probably smaller than they feel. It usually helps.
